author: @DuardoEh
text:
G'day Mr. Murray-Smith,
I can't think of a better person to share this decades-old idea with.
It seemed to me that the Starlight story appeared around the time of the mad-cow BSE scare in Britain decades ago. Though possibly wrong about the timing, as soon as I read that prions are indestructible I made the connection to Starlight.
A search a few years ago hinted that prions are being manufactured.
Has someone (else) made the connection and layered misformed protein (prions) into their Starlight sandwich or other concoction? Paint? Structure?
Food for thought?
NASA?

author: @OriginalMorningStar
text:
I had a bit of a play with a Starlite type formulation and discovered your interesting note about non-reticulated foams for myself. I added Perlite to mine to make what I called Starcrete... Perlite is a volcanic foam comprised mainly of silica, millions of tiny glassy bubbles formed while it was molten liquid. Its very similar to Meerschaum, which floats on water. Perlite doesnt float, but it does hold significant amounts of water like a sponge, and is used in horticulture to improve water retention in soils and composts. I figured if I replaced the cavities in it with Starlite, it would behave like a super-lightweight concrete with built-in intumescence that could be used as a building material rather than a paint. It worked rather well, but I never had a use for it.
I also have a reputation for being inventive across a wide spectrum, and thats because I'm self-taught, recognising that protocols engender linear thinking and that if wanted anything different then breaking protocols was the way to go. Experimentation is the mother of invention, necessity is its father.

author: @TonyLorenzano
text:
Hey, Robert! I was loving it all the way untl you niped Tesla. Leave my man, Tesla alone an go watch Edison electrocute Topsy, The Baby elephantwith AC! LOL!

I used to love Edison, too, but I believe he was not that nice to his employees or business partners, especial those that did the most to make him rich, it would seem. (Tesla.) [Spencer Tracer was charming; It don't think Edison was!] Poor Tesla had a 50 cent per horsepower royalty on his motor with (friend) Westinghouse, whose now hugely successful business was failing, (probably because he was expanding into services and products for elevators and railroad, which Westinghouse still is active in today.) 

Tesla was not so much a savant, as he was an example of the optimum properties of the human brain/mind.

Lots of well thought out, experiments. Among other things, he said that his mind worked in a way that he could do several related experiments at once, in his mind visuallly, viscerally, and leave them on a shelf for a few weeks... up to something like 12 projects at the same time. Some of them he claimed he could hold in his mind, as though they were plugged in and operating, then come back in a coupe of weeks to check the metal parts for wear. (He also that these abilities were so sharpened that in his early years he had extrasensory experiences on ocassions.) 

Edisons grounding in electricitywas far from good and based on the survival od DC at the cost of our AC exitence! Think about Edison controlling your power grid and no Tesla to bail him out!

Look around. I helped someone lift into a car, a heavy "pressed wood" RCA console, from the late 1940s. In perfect condition-- It was so  pretty that I hated thinking of it junk! (Our country has banned his incandenscent lightbulbs, you know.) And, we don't do motion picture "films" his way anymore, do we?


Every thing electric is either Tesla or NOT-Edison! Someone smarter than him could have made pulsed DC well as our present AC standard which is similiar to parts of the UK. But, not Edison! Not even in 50 years.

And, I believe that it's clear we cut Tesla off at the knees at some point, when he had more unique ideas to offer us.
His buddy Einstein called him... was it... "the smartest man on Earth"?  [Many of us interest in physics note that as amazing as Eainstein was he made two three BIG mistakes. Einstein admits that about his Cosmological (fudge factor) constant around 1920?  And spooky entanglement of paired things at a quantum level. Tesla criticised this and other things about his understanding of electromagnetism and gravity. 

There is a dynamic movment in modernday physics to rewrite it, all the way back to Einstein's days in the Swiss patent office. 
(Of course he will remain a pillar in physics, but there must be radical change, immediately---BIG-College-Money-Science.


Tesla has given us everything electrical, and now we are anxiously turning back to him for even more understanding! 
The fax mchine turned obsolete in 2000. A surprising number of cell phone users don't know who Alexander Graham Bell was! And, Edison?... The lightbulb is all that comes to mind out of his (800?) inventions... movies?, too...

But, Tesla!

And, the the pigeons thing, again!  LOOK! By all accounts he was a very good person,who would have labor for us, even if it didn't reward him with fame or money. (Not so sure about Edison's ego!) Fame, money, booze, women, rivers champagne and the next day, back to his lab, to invent some radio devices to communicate with extraterrestrials. It cut him some slack.  [I wonder if he ever had sex? I don't remember any story about that!] 



He died old and senile and alone and unders heavy suspicion and a veil of conspiracy. Within hours, J. Edgar Hoover's boys had rifled his  safe, some things are supposedly still secret after say 100 years!

author: @VeniceInventors
text:
I like the analogy of inventions and gold, which ties in very nicely with the need for a team.
Building a team without any funding is rather difficult, but wearing all the hats is even more difficult.
Any inventor (artists too) should definitely set some time aside to learn about business, because most projects can't succeed without proper funding.
An alternative for those of us who just can't stand the boring reality of business, is to find someone who actually enjoys it, and is willing to invest time for a fair share of future earnings.

author: @VoidySA
text: Necessity is the mother of invention, You're more likely to find an answer if you actually need to do something and trying things instead of wanting to do something and wondering about it all day. I believe actually experiencing a result communicates much more clearly everything that's going on than reading a book about it. Research is important but it's only a tool helping you to understand what are actually happening with your experiment. The moment something else happens that are not written about in research papers, that's the lightbulb moment.

author: @chaorrottai
text:
"just knowing something can be done..."

The refrigeration cycle is my motivation, the refrigeration cycle says to me that more energy can be relocated against a gradient than is required to relocate the energy.

I believe that the priciples that govern the efficiency of the refrigeration cycle are applicable in more scope than just refrigeration and that the same logic can be applied to other forms of energy manipulation/trasport.

I think of typical electric generators as an anaolgy for a friction heater and then look for the analogy for the heat pump

"err, um no..."

author: @MathewTitus
text:
What I have found in all creative people one quality that is fundamental to coming up with successful ideas - courage. 
University creates a culture of fear of failure. And that is why so few actually come up with something new and interesting.
And courage is something you simply cannot teach. You either have it or you don't.
Also, those who don't know anything about the dangers that lie in a dark forest - are most likely the ones that willingly wander into it.

 Cheers Robert 😊

author: @stevecummins324
text: I've mucked around with using a starlight esq "dough" as lining for a DIY  carbon arc furnace. Nothing else that was easily available seemed to be able to stand up to heat of that. Which has me wondering if use of such mixtures could date right back to the early bronze age!

author: @stephenroot1012
text: If one wants a source of books for experiments like this one can do worse than some standard phone books. They are available for free in our area from the local telephone /internet service provider. Just claim that you are opening up a new office if they ask why you are grabbing a bunch of them and in our area they get replaced every year so if you are careful and quick you can get a bunch of them at once when they throw the old ones out.
